Title: The miR408a-BBP-LAC3/CSD1 module regulates anthocyanin biosynthesis mediated by crosstalk between copper homeostasis and ROS homeostasis during light induction in Malus plants

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; contributes to red coloration and ROS scavenging
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: miRNA-seq in mature leaves of 'Royalty' and 'Flame'
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by light in apple fruit peel; increased under Cu deficiency; decreased under excess Cu; increased from S2 during 'Royalty' leaf development
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light induction after bag removal; copper deficiency (-Cu); excess copper (1-100 μM CuSO4)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. miR408a was identified as a positive regulator of anthocyanin accumulation in Malus. 2. Transient overexpression of miR408a increased anthocyanin content, Cu content, and expression of anthocyanin-pathway genes, whereas silencing caused the opposite effects. 3. miR408a cleaves MdBBP transcripts and modulates the BBP-LAC3/CSD1 module linking Cu homeostasis and ROS homeostasis to anthocyanin biosynthesis under light induction and Cu stress.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: miRNA-seq; RLM-RACE cleavage validation; transient overexpression; TRV-mediated silencing; qRT-PCR; HPLC anthocyanin measurement; ICP Cu quantification
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanin accumulation and apple/leaf red coloration; increases Cu content
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for improving red coloration and anthocyanin content in apple through transient or stable activation, but Cu-homeostasis effects should be monitored under deficiency or toxicity conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Upstream regulator MdMYB16 binds the miR408a promoter, but MdMYB16 is not itself a core gene of this study. miR408a is a regulatory small RNA rather than a TF or enzyme, therefore classified here as Common Gene.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S constitutive promoter for overexpression; TRV vector for silencing

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; pigment contributing to red coloration
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Downregulated by light induction; upregulated under excess Cu; downregulated under Cu deficiency; decreases during leaf development from S2
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light induction; Cu deficiency; excess Cu
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdBBP is a cleavable target of miR408a. 2. Overexpression of MdBBP decreased Cu content and anthocyanin accumulation, while BBP silencing increased both. 3. MdBBP interacts with MdLAC3 and MdCSD1 and acts as a central negative regulator linking Cu homeostasis/ROS homeostasis with anthocyanin biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RLM-RACE; transient overexpression; TRV-mediated silencing; Y2H; BiFC; subcellular localization; qRT-PCR; HPLC anthocyanin measurement; ICP Cu quantification
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negatively regulates Cu homeostasis and anthocyanin biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess BBP reduces coloration and Cu content; implicated in lignin/phenylpropanoid shifts
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suppression of BBP may be useful to enhance anthocyanin-based fruit coloration, but effects on Cu distribution, ROS balance, and lignin metabolism should be considered
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BBP did not directly bind promoters of CHS, DFR, UFGT, F3OH, or FLS in Y1H. Authors propose BBP may function as a Cu sensor/shuttle between membrane, cytoplasm, and vacuole.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: basic blue protein; plantacyanin subclass of phytocyanin family; cupredoxin superfamily; type I blue copper protein; transmembrane helix; signal peptide
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S constitutive promoter for overexpression; TRV vector for silencing

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: lignin biosynthesis / phenylpropanoid polymerization; indirectly linked to anthocyanin regulation via Cu homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: downstream/terminal polymerization step in lignin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes lignin monomers to promote their polymerization into lignin macromolecular heteropolymers
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: lignin polymers/radicals leading to lignin polymerization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product branch; modulates Cu-binding/homeostasis linked to anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by light according to abstract/conclusion context but reduced in apple pericarp during light treatment figure text; increased under excess Cu; decreased under Cu deficiency; parallels BBP during leaf development
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light induction; Cu deficiency; excess Cu
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdLAC3 physically interacts with MdBBP in Y2H and BiFC assays. 2. MdLAC3 expression is positively associated with BBP and responds to Cu and light conditions. 3. Authors place MdLAC3 in a miR408a-BBP-LAC3/CSD1 module linking Cu homeostasis and anthocyanin/lignin metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Y2H; BiFC; qRT-PCR correlation under miR408a/BBP manipulation and Cu/light treatments
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with Cu homeostasis changes that influence anthocyanin accumulation; also linked to lignin metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct genetic manipulation of MdLAC3 alone was reported; role in anthocyanin is indirect through Cu-homeostasis module.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Predicted cell membrane and extracellular; interaction with BBP observed on cell membrane/tonoplast context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential co-target in engineering Cu-homeostasis/phenylpropanoid balance, but direct causality to anthocyanin remains module-based

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: ROS detoxification / Cu-Zn superoxide dismutation, indirectly linked to anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts superoxide radicals into oxygen and hydrogen peroxide, contributing to ROS detoxification
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for homeostatic environment supporting target product accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Increased by light induction; increased under excess Cu; decreased under Cu deficiency; parallels BBP during leaf development
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light induction; Cu deficiency; excess Cu
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdCSD1 physically interacts with MdBBP in Y2H and BiFC assays. 2. MdCSD1 responds to light and Cu conditions and is proposed to connect Cu homeostasis with ROS homeostasis. 3. The miR408a-BBP-CSD1 branch contributes to anthocyanin accumulation indirectly by modulating ROS status under light induction and Cu stress.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Y2H; BiFC; subcellular localization; qRT-PCR correlation under miR408a/BBP manipulation and Cu/light treatments
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports anthocyanin accumulation indirectly through ROS-homeostasis regulation coupled to Cu homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct manipulation of MdCSD1 alone was reported; effect on anthocyanin is inferred through the module. Fig./text indicate increased CSD1 during light induction despite some inconsistent wording for other Cu-binding genes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary target for balancing ROS detoxification with pigmentation under Cu/light stress, but direct engineering evidence is limited
